planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor, rocky soil in full sun to partial shade; new Jersey tea has an extremely deep taproot and is very difficult to transplant once established — site it carefully the first time.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to poor, rocky soil in full sun to partial shade; New Jersey tea is a remarkable native shrub with one of the most interesting histories in American botany — the dried leaves were widely used as a tea substitute during the Revolutionary War when British tea was boycotted. The fluffy clusters of tiny white flowers in early summer are among the most important nectar sources in the native prairie and woodland edge, attracting dozens of bee species, skippers, and hairstreak butterflies. The roots fix atmospheric nitrogen, improving soil fertility over time. This shrub is exceptionally drought-tolerant once its deep taproot is established, thriving on rocky, infertile, dry soils where few shrubs succeed. The spring bee value alone makes it an outstanding native garden plant. It resents being moved after establishment, so site it correctly from the start.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in very well-drained, dry to moderately moist, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline soil in partial shade to full sun; fragrant sumac is one of the best native shrubs for dry, shaded slopes and is excellent for erosion control.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, dry to moderately moist, slightly acidic to slightly alkaline soil in partial shade to full sun; fragrant sumac is one of the most versatile, adaptable, and underused native shrubs in North America — tolerating dry shade, rocky slopes, alkaline soil, and drought conditions that defeat most plants. The crushed leaves have a distinctly pleasant lemon-citrus fragrance. Small yellow flowers in early spring before the leaves emerge provide important early nectar for native bees emerging from overwintering. The fuzzy red fruit clusters feed dozens of bird species through fall and winter. The three-parted leaves turn brilliant orange-red and burgundy in fall — among the best native plants for fall color — and the low, spreading form makes it excellent for groundcover use on slopes. The cultivar 'Gro-Low' is a compact, 2-foot-tall groundcover form widely used in landscape planting.

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or container vines in spring in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; yellow passionflower is the most shade-tolerant of the native Passiflora species and naturalizes readily in shaded woodland edges and fence lines in zone 6–9.

directions: Plant in moist to average, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial to full shade; yellow passionflower is the most shade-tolerant native Passiflora species and is far less aggressive than maypop (P. incarnata), making it more suitable for smaller gardens and shaded areas. The flowers are smaller than other passionflowers but share the same intricate, layered structure — creamy yellow-green petals and a distinctive corona of filaments — and are visited by long-tongued native bees for nectar. Like all native Passiflora, yellow passionflower is a host plant for the larvae of gulf fritillary, variegated fritillary, and zebra longwing butterflies. The small dark purple fruits are eaten by birds. It grows naturally along shaded woodland edges, thickets, and roadsides throughout the southeastern US and is one of the best native vines for adding butterfly-host value to a shaded fence or trellis.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs or root cuttings in spring in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, alkaline to neutral, sandy to rocky soil in full sun; leadplant has an extraordinarily deep root system (to 16 feet) and is extremely drought tolerant once established. Do not overwater.

directions: Plant in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, neutral to slightly alkaline, sandy or rocky soil in full sun; leadplant is a keystone prairie shrub considered by ecologists one of the best indicators of an intact, high-quality tallgrass prairie — its presence signals deep, healthy, undisturbed prairie soil, since its roots penetrate up to 16 feet deep. The silver-gray, pinnately compound leaves (with a lead-gray, canescent coating of dense fine hairs) are beautiful year-round and give the plant its name. The spikes of deep violet-purple flowers with bright orange anthers in summer are exceptional for native bumblebees and specialist bees. It is a nitrogen-fixing legume that improves soil fertility. The combination of extreme drought tolerance, nitrogen-fixing roots, and high bee value makes leadplant one of the most ecologically important prairie shrubs for habitat restoration and dry, sunny gardens.

planting.note: Plant divisions or slips 18–24 inches apart in rows along the contour of a slope in full sun; vetiver is sterile and does not self-sow, making it non-invasive in spite of its vigorous growth. Established plants tolerate drought, flooding, and extreme heat.

directions: Plant slips or divisions along contour lines on slopes in full sun in any well-drained soil; vetiver is the world standard for low-cost erosion control on steep slopes — a tropical grass from South Asia with an extraordinary root system that penetrates 6–10 feet straight down (not laterally) and holds soil with remarkable efficiency against both water and wind erosion. The World Bank promotes vetiver hedgerow systems in developing countries as the most effective and affordable slope stabilization technology available. The aromatic roots are distilled to produce vetiver essential oil, a base note used in hundreds of fine perfumes. Unlike most grasses used for erosion control, vetiver is sterile and non-invasive — it produces no viable seed and spreads only by division. In zone 8–11 it is an outstanding low-maintenance erosion control plant for steep slopes, stream banks, and road cuts.
